The OpenEye way to deal with such molecules is to timeout after 60s (the timeout can be changed via a command-line option).
I.e. if a calculation fails to complete within 60s for one molecule, the molecule is logged as an error, and the software continues to process further molecules.
I think this is a quite smart approach, since you never really know what you will encounter when you work with >100k molecules.
And you don't want the processing to stop just because of one faulty molecule in the big batch that you were intending to process.

里面的print改成send_to_ssdb()或者是扔进一个内存数据结构broker中

```python 
import threading

timer=threading.Timer(600,ip_job.put({'fn':send_to_redis/ssdb(select_best(ip_addr))})

然而这样会阻塞主进程,因为timer虽然是异步的,但是10分钟的任务结构是会阻塞的

PS: Timer是可以做一个自我循环的,但是那个进程有点不稳定,容易自我死亡,还是再开一个线程,做一个阻塞的任务比较好

所以再开一个任务队列:

```python
import QUANTAXIS as QA
import queue
task_job=queue.Queue()
task_=QA.QA_Queue(task_job)
task_.setName('task_sche')
task_.start()
def ten_min_job():
    timer=threading.Timer(600,ip_job.put({'fn':send_to_redis/ssdb(select_best(ip_addr))})
    time.sleep(600)
    
    ten_min_job()
task_job.put({'fn':ten_min_job()})
```

> What I understand and don't understand are:
>  "images" are a collection or list of images.

yes, images are a list of numpy arrays of shape (H,W,C) or a single numpy array of shape (N,H,W,C).

> "seq = iaa.Sequential(...)", "seg" is a defined augmenters list and the defined operation sequence will be applied on each image in images. But because "random_order=True" and some parameters in some augmenter in seq are random, I think the transformation sequence applied on each image is different.

yes

> "images_aug = seq.augment_images(images)", In this section, I got augmented images.

yes

> If I want to known what augment operations are applied on each image in images, how should I code?

That's currently not really possible in the library. One could hack a bit with hooks to find out which augmenters were executed, but also estimating which parameters were used and which samples were drawn from them would be non-trivial and probably require quite some monkey-patching.

> I cannot find function "augment_images" had been called,like as "images_aug = seq.augment_images(images)". So , I think the sentence "seq.show_grid(images[0], cols=8, rows=8)" call augment operation. Is it true?

yes

> But I am confused that: We have only 16 pictures in original images, why we can get 64 images after calling seq.show_grid? One image and one seq, get one new image, Isn't it?

The function `show_grid()` receives (a) one or more input images, (b) the number of columns of images to draw (per image from (a) ), (c) the number of rows of images to draw (per image from (a) ). So if columns C=8 and rows R=8 then `C*R = 8*8 = 64` images are drawn per image from (a). I.e. `show_grid(images[0], cols=8, rows=8)` draws 64 images as it gets a single input image (the first one in `images`). All drawn images are augmented versions of `images[0]`. The augmentation sequence `seq` is simply executed as often as necessary to get to `C*R`. That `images` contains 16 images here doesn't matter. It could also contain 1000 images and it wouldn't change anything, as show_grid only receives the first image from `images` (and in the second call it receives the first and second image).

I'd consider the Curio support to be preliminary.  Needs more testing.  Still thinking about certain facets of the API.   For example, to be consistent with the rest of Curio, should methods like `connect()`, `bind()`, `close()` and such be defined as `async`?    Should the sockets support the async-context management protocol?

Right now, it does not allow any combination of nesting.   So something like disable->enable->enable is illegal and results in a RuntimeError.   disable->enable->disable->enable is okay though.  

Here is my general thought on this... cancellation control is a pretty advanced thing to be introducing into your code.   It makes everything more difficult to reason about and requires a LOT of attention to fine details.  Because of that, its use really requires a fair amount of forethought.  My general fear is that people who don't quite understand what they're doing would start using these features haphazardly.  For example, writing a bunch of library functions like this:

    async def coro():
            async with enable_cancellation():
                   ...

IMO, just having a bare `enable_cancellation()` sitting there at the top of functions like that would have the potential to cause all kinds of insanity elsewhere.   By disallowing the use of `enable_cancellation()` all by itself, I think it will "force" people to use it in closer proximity to `disable_cancellation()`--most likely in the same function.   My hope is that this would produce greater clarity about what is actually happening with respect to cancellation control when these features are being used. 

From an implementation standpoint, the restriction is somewhat artificial. There's nothing about the implementation that nesting breaks.  The risk of breakage is more with user-written code that happens to use these features.

IPV6_V6ONLY is a flag you can set on ipv6 sockets (family=AF_INET6) to
control whether the same socket can also sorta-transparently speak ipv4.
You may or may not want this, depending on your v4/v6 implementation
strategy. (For example, a server could have a single ipv4+ipv6 listening
socket, but then if it logs IP addresses that people are connecting from it
needs to be prepared to handle "ipv4 mapped addresses"; for some
applications maybe it's simpler to instead have two listening sockets, one
for v4 and one for v6.)

> Unfortunately and astonishingly a real solution for reliably doing kernel-supported non-blocking file IO under UNIX / Linux still does not exist (...and AIO sucks). It appears it should possible on BSD via kqueue(). Funnily Windows is the only one who managed to do this right.

This used to bother me a lot more until I realized that from the kernel point of view, one-thread-per-I/O-request is basically isomorphic to what Windows is doing. Basically you have to store the temporary state involved in keeping track of a half-finished I/O operation somewhere. Windows does this explicitly with some sort of manually maintained data structures that it switches between manually; Linux uses the C stack. If you're a kernel, though, then the C stack itself basically is just another data structure that you switch between... so the "just spawn a kernel thread for each request" approach described in that LWN article is basically as good as anything. How does this compare to a user-space threadpool? User-space threads are a little more expensive to context-switch between. And they take up user memory for their stack. But on a lazy-allocating system like Linux, this is only ~1 page per thread in practice, which is nothing for a reasonably sized thread pool. And they take up virtual address space, but on a 64-bit system there's plenty to spare. For programs that are close to the metal and pushing the limits of the hardware, these kinds of small overheads do matter... but for Python programs I doubt the kernel/user-space thread distinction is important.

To be clear to anyone reading this the correct approach is to:

1. Register the users salt and verifier over TSL/SSL to protect the verifier against being intercepted for an offline brute force attack. Also you want to protect the user id which if known can allow for online guessing attacks trying 'obvious' passwords: so use TSL/SSL for both the reiteration and the login for extra protection. 
2. Bonus marks use a password meter widget to show the user if their password is string to encourage them to use passwords which cannot be guessed quickly with such an online attack. 
3. Use a session key which should be a hash of the SRP 'S' value as the AES key. That's the shared secret. AES also needs a random value so that you can use the same key multiple times by salting the key for each usage. That prevents the user uploading lots of similar data which have common bit patterns which leak info about the key. In WWII we jammed enemy comms to force them to retransmit using the same key just to use this attack to crack their codes. So every use of a shared key needs a unique random publicly shared salt to protect the shared key. 

End.

Yes, it's UTC and it's how it is supposed to be!

Let me try to clarify this with an example: the "lifecycle" of weather forecasts on Beijing (UTC+8) - let's take a forecast for day 2017-04-30

  1. OpenWeatherMap API computes the weather forecast for day 2017-04-12. As forecasts need to have  a reference timestamp and its format is not cut to only YYYY-MM-DD then hours minutes and seconds need to be specified: therefore, I guess OWM API takes midday as the reference timestamp, which is 2017-04-30 12:00:00+8 in local Beijing time, 2017-04-30 04:00:00+0 in UTC
  2. The forecast is delivered as a JSON document in response to your application's request. This carries the UTC timestamp (2017-04-30 04:00:00+0)
  3. PyOWM reads that JSON, builds the Forecast object and gives you that object. It does not change anything on the timestamps, that still are expressed in UTC.

Then,  if you want to have them in your local timezone, you need to bring back the UTC timestamps to local Beijing's timezone (UTC+8) - **but that's up to you**

How can you do it? Use [pytz](http://pytz.sourceforge.net), it's super easy!

Hope this helped :)

yas3fs uses the regular Boto 2 library under the hood which has this support baked in.

If you don't specify any credentials (and keep Boto from finding them in the filesystem), Boto will grab the EC2 role credentials from the metadata endpoint automatically (and properly refreshing them when needed). In other words, you shouldn't need to do anything here as long as you've launched your EC2 instance with a properly configured IAM role.

In fact, passing `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID` and `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` from the metadata endpoint wouldn't be sufficient. You would also need the token code, passed in `AWS_SECURITY_TOKEN`.

The [Boto 2 documentation](http://boto.cloudhackers.com/en/latest/boto_config_tut.html#credentials) is incorrect in this regard. The [Boto 3 documentation](http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guide/configuration.html#configuring-credentials) is correct for both versions of the library.

The gory details in Boto 2 can be found in [boto/provider.py](https://github.com/boto/boto/blob/develop/boto/provider.py#L278), starting around line 278. It finally gets to the metadata endpoint [at line 375](https://github.com/boto/boto/blob/develop/boto/provider.py#L278).

I think I've found all the remaining issues.  First, apparently find_package() on windows has the special behavior of searching recent build folders.  So if you build dlib somewhere and then try to compile the example programs with a CMakeLists.txt file that uses `find_package(dlib)` it fill find the dlibConfig.cmake file in your recent build folder and try to use it. But that results in an error on the line that says `    include("${dlib_CMAKE_DIR}/dlib.cmake")` since that isn't the right path.  I'm sure this error would confuse windows users so it should be addressed.  The best thing to do is probably to put the config files inside some sub-folder rather than right in the cmake binary dir, so find_package() won't see them.  

The only other remaining thing is to add the missing #define switches into config.h.  I.e. any #defines set by dlib/CMakeLists.txt need to be echoed into the config.h file.  So this includes DLIB_ENABLE_STACK_TRACE, DLIB_NO_GUI_SUPPORT, and DLIB_ISO_CPP_ONLY.

Other than those things I think the PR is ready :)

It helps to have lists or numpy arrays (with missing values as `nan`) when we need to do some array transformations or matrix multiplication, on the result.

I agree OpenCV API is not the best, for example it would have been useful to override the numpy array and keep color space information somewhere. Pandas does a great job of re-using numpy arrays, so that performance is not hit but extra information is used. That is of course much more work. 

In general in Python it will help tremendously if all objects are first-class (or derived from one) because many libraries will work with them out of the box. If they are some sort of container for example, it helps to have `__iter__` and indexing, for standard python functions ('map', 'filter', ..) as well as other libraries (scikit-learn, nvd3, ...) to be able to use them.

Of course, the API is most important and should be well thought out, but the current API (for Python) can benefit from some of Python's strengths. Perhaps we can ask more Ptyhon users to see what they think.

@heitzmann We have now studied the hashing of .gds files extensively and have reached the following conclusions:

1. Binary .gds files generated by the **identical python code** using the same versions of gdspy, numpy, python, etc often result in binary .gds files that are **different**. This difference remains even when timestamps are taken out of .gds files. The difference persists even when running using the same software/hardware (the same computer).

2. **The fact that the binary files are different does not mean that the resultant geometries are different**. We have used boolean comparison across platforms and are satisfied that the geometries are the same with some residual differences related to number rounding.

3. As the complexity of layouts increases, parallelization is becoming more prevalent. Order of execution hinders parallelization. In complex layouts, the sequence of assembly of geometrical elements (and their fracture) is not expected to be retained if the benefits of parallelization are to be reaped.

This leads us to the following conclusion. Employing hashing algorithms without considering the logic of what is being hashed is not possible in .gds files. As far as hashing is concerned, there does not appear to be a simple solution here, short of looking at the most generic case of data entropy suggested in the link provided by @explorer2011. Of course, there is always a possibility of comparing two geometries using boolean geometry operations, though we have found this computationally impractical for our complex layouts.

I agree that passing un-sanitized strings as endpoint specifiers may indeed result in surprising behavior.

Any code that uses Twisted endpoints would end up passing the strings to ``clientFromString`` or ``serverFromString`` and receive back an ``IStreamClientEndpoint`` (or ``IStreamServerEndpoint``). You could then whitelist acceptable endpoint subclasses.

Alternatively, you could whitelist acceptable prefix-strings, since (I believe) this is all Twisted is doing to identify parsers. So ``endpointstring.split(':')[0] in whitelist`` would be sufficient to check. Looking at the returned object would obviously be slightly better, as from the string-checking you still don't know precisely which parser Twisted will call.

only natural amino-acids (and nucleic acids) are marked as ATOM, and the modified ones are marked as HETATM.

Looked into this more closely; supporting alternative distance functions is a bit of a pain.  I think for now the best approach is to modify the signature so that `distance=None` implies `|x-y|`, for which we can do the fast bisection search.  Any non-None metric supplied (eg mod-n distance used in multipitch) will revert to the current outer-distance method.

`#!/usr/bin/env python
from __future__ import print_function
import pybedtools
import argparse
import os
import sys
import multiprocessing

def featuretype_filter(feature, featuretype):
   
    if feature[2] == featuretype:
        return True
    return False

def subset_featuretypes(featuretype):
    return g.filter(featuretype_filter, featuretype).saveas()


def count_reads_in_features(features):
    return features.intersect(abam=bam,
                             b=features.fn,
                             s=stranded,
                             bed=True,
                             stream=True).count()
def main():
    
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser(prog=os.path.basename(sys.argv[0]),
                                 usage=__doc__)
    ap.add_argument('--gff', required=True,
                    help='GFF or GTF file containing annotations')
    ap.add_argument('--bam', required=True,
                    help='BAM file containing reads to be counted')
    ap.add_argument('--stranded', action='store_true',
                    help='Use strand-specific merging and overlap. '
                         'Default is to ignore strand')
    ap.add_argument('--no-parallel', dest='noparallel', action='store_true',
                    help='Disables parallel computation')
    ap.add_argument('-o', '--output',
                    help='Optional file to which results will be written; '
                         'default is stdout')
    ap.add_argument('-v', '--verbose', action='store_true',
                    help='Verbose (goes to stderr)')
    args = ap.parse_args()

    gff = args.gff
    bam = args.bam
    stranded = args.stranded
    parallel = not args.noparallel

    # Some GFF files have invalid entries -- like chromosomes with negative
    # coords or features of length = 0.  This line removes them and saves the
    # result in a tempfile
    g = pybedtools.BedTool(gff).remove_invalid().saveas()

    # Decide which version of map to use.  If parallel, we only need 3
    # processes.
    pool = multiprocessing.Pool(processes=3)

    # Get separate files for introns and exons in parallel (if specified)
    featuretypes = ('intron', 'exon')
    introns, exons = pool.map(subset_featuretypes, featuretypes)

    # Perform some genome algebra to get unique and shared regions
    exon_only = exons.subtract(introns).merge().remove_invalid().saveas()
    intron_only = introns.subtract(exons).merge().remove_invalid().saveas()
    intron_and_exon = exons\
            .intersect(introns).merge().remove_invalid().saveas()

    # Do intersections with BAM file in parallel
    features = (exon_only, intron_only, intron_and_exon)
    results = pool.map(count_reads_in_features, features)

    labels = ('      exon only:',
              '    intron only:',
              'intron and exon:')

    for label, reads in zip(labels, results):
        print('%s %s' % (label, reads))

    pybedtools.cleanup(verbose=False)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()`

> Although I think that having the ability to add a prefix - preferably simply by adding a path to the base URL - is a good idea in general, and would be necessary in a more complicated setup than mine

The full request path in Django is always determined by ``SCRIPT_NAME`` (external path prefix outside of Django's control, e.g. reverse proxies) plus the full Django route. This was the reasoning behind giving no such "force" mechanism in ``drf-yasg``, since there is already a "force" mechanism in Django for external paths (FORCE_SCRIPT_NAME), and full internal routes are always available to be passed into the schema view at an appropriate location.

I might reconsider this if someone provides an example of such a setup where passing full urlpatterns is not possible or desirable, but for now I would consider the implicit mechanisms sufficient.

Assuming tests pass, I think this is ready to go. It provides a complete port of the java client's metric system, minus the JMX reporter. I haven't done much of the instrumentation, but have instrumented fetch requests and the consumer coordinator as a first pass example. My plan is to ship this with the next release, and then soon thereafter work on matching the java client's instrumentation.

Important: This doesn't publicly expose the metrics we're collecting just yet. Because the java client relies on JMX as the default built-in reporter, we need to make a design decision about how to handle that in kafka-python. I think a simple dictionary based approach would make sense, but would like to mess around with that some more before committing to a public interface. Towards this end I've made DictReporter that can export a snapshot of all stats at the current time and their values. That said, users can easily provide their own reporters through configuration to expose the metrics, just like in the java implementation. I've updated the documentation on `KafkaConsumer` to reflect this.

#424 and #505 both add some metric instrumentation and provide a couple ways of retrieving those metrics - one exposes a dictionary of metrics, and the other has a handler system. This PR provides the same reporter interface the java client provides, which I think is similar to the handler system in #505. My intent for the DictReporter is to make the default instance of it available through a public API similar to #424. I don't know if the java implementation has the same instrumentation as the two mentioned PRs, but I would plan to mirror the java instrumentation fairly closely, but there's no reason we couldn't add our own instrumentation if it made sense.

> Hi @teor2345, thank you for the PR. Can you tell me what your use-case is for this functionality? Where do you load the ipasn_string from?

We run a cooperating group of data collecting instances on different machines.
They receive their configuration from a central point.

Here is the use case in detail:
1. The central point reads the config from a file and checks it is valid using pyasn
2. The central point reads the file into a string, and sends the string along with many other config strings to the data collecting instances
3. The data collecting instances read the config from the string, and load it using pyasn

This avoids the data collecting instances dumping the string to a file (they have no ability to write files) and then reading it out again using pyasn. It would be slower and more error-prone to write a file (for example, if the directory wasn't writeable, or the disk was full).

After some debugging I figured out why this is occurring, it's due to the hash function in Style only checking the hash for Font and Fill, so the equality constraints are never checked since Style is a parent class for these. That is, Font and other type classes needs to be checked for hash and equality, but the primitive properties can still satisfy the half hash half equality behavior.

I think a reasonable fix is to hash all the non-primitive children in Style and all other parent-containing classes as this retains the performance benefit. I'll get a fix for this in a bit, thanks for finding it, this is quite an obscure bug. :)

Ok, so I ended up making a few internal changes.

* The `ajaxError` callback now includes a `settings` parameter, which previously was not included from the jQuery [`.ajaxError()` method](http://api.jquery.com/ajaxError/)

    ```js
    ajaxError: function( config, xhr, settings, exception ){ return exception; };
    ```

    I entered it between the `xhr` and `exception` parameter to keep the same order as the parameters returned by jQuery.

* The `ajaxError` callback is now *always* called, including times when the `$.tablesorter.showError()` function is not called by the internal ajax error method.
* If the `ajaxError` callback returns an empty string, it will be essentially the same as `return false` in that all error rows will be removed.
* I guess I didn't explicitly mention this in the docs, but the message returned by the `ajaxError` function can include HTML, or even the HTML for the entire row to be added as an error row to the `thead`.
This is in the documentation that is being prepped for release.

The file my.dcm is 20 bytes long and contains:
```
[ Tag           ][ OF  ]            [length of 8] [ float 1.0   ]  [ float 2.0   ]    
00 56 20 00 4F 46 00 00 08 00 00 00 00 00 80 3F 00 00 00 40
```

dcmdump displays it correctly as:
`(5600,0020) OF 1\2                                      #   8, 1 SpectroscopyData`

So, it looks like it is encoded correctly at the dicom file as 2 x 32 bit floats without any delimiter (dcmdump implicitly adds the delimiter  - but shows the VM as 1).

However, you are correct that:
`
fm.get_item(0x56000020).VM`

is set to '2'.  The DICOM standard at the end of [section 6.4
](http://dicom.nema.org/DICOM/2013/output/chtml/part05/sect_6.4.html) implies that the VM of an "OF" VR is always "1", but I am not sure i can figure out a scenario where pydicom should insist that VM for these should always be "1" when the value for this tag returned by pydiocm is a list of floats just as if the VR were "FL".

As far as I can tell from a brief read of the standard, the only difference between "OF" and "FL" as a VR is that OF has a 32 bit long length field at all times and FL only has a 16 bit length field with an explicit VR syntax.  Otherwise, they are encoded exactly the same.  

Is there a scenario where having the VM set to other than 1 for "OF" is a problem if the encoded file is still correct?

Unfortunately using the LiveServerTestCase means that mocking will have no effect. The app object is being deployed via the `run()` method and can't be modified once it has been run. You can think about this like if you ran the development server -- changes that you make to the code don't take effect (unless you're using the reloader which is a different mechanism entirely) until re-running it.

In general its better to use the regular TestCase with the fake client if you are planning on mocking. In your case it doesn't seem like that is possible. However, there are a couple of solutions that I can think of depending on what the rest of that code looks like

1. When you create your `ThirdPartyApi` use the configuration environment to determine whether to instantiate the real one or some fake one (whatever that fake implementation looks like)
1. I don't recommend this method, but it is possible: in the create_app method, you can monkey patch the third party api if it is attached to app via a property (eg. app.third_party_api) and set it to a new implementation

Hi,

This is expected behaviour - pyangbind only builds classes for instantiated schema nodes, not for logical groupings. 

The bindings are generated in the schema where they are actually instantiated - that is to say the turing machine module itself will have the `tape-content-2` etc. leaves added, rather than them being generated for `second_tape`.

Incidentally, the same is true for a `grouping`. Bindings are generated only when a `uses` statement references a grouping.

If you have further questions, please re-open the issue.

@melnikaite That may be a separate issue.  The code @yehudahkay posted (and the code I tested) loops over several blobs and calls `saveAs` on each.  If you look here: https://github.com/eligrey/FileSaver.js/blob/master/FileSaver.js#L146 you will see that the `href` and `download` attributes for the `save_link` element is set then `setTimeout` is called.  By the time the `setTimeout` callback is invoked  `href` and `download` have been overwritten multiple times and only the last file is downloaded.

The patch submitted (https://github.com/Hypercubed/FileSaver.js/commit/04f232f658b0d70cad0a2bebf5a514de2deab40a) fixes this issue by moving the setting `href` and `download` into the `setTimeout` callback.  See here: http://plnkr.co/edit/rVuId9?p=info (saveAs is used indirectly).

        elif isinstance(node.ctx, (ast.Param, ast.Store)):
            self._define_variable(node.id, node.lineno)

We would need separate lists for "defined variables" and "defined parameters" and split the "elif" into two "elifs" for ast.Store and ast.Param. However, I don't see an easy way for distinguishing between "used variable" and "used parameter". Therefore, we can't detect unused parameters.

Use of proxy for modulo doesn't even work in all cases when using C API either. Current thinking is that for nb_power case, shouldn't even unwrap the second argument, let alone modulo, thereby ensuring consistent behaviour, even if means can't pass proxy as second or third arguments.

Lets look at the combinations and how things work.

For pure Python code with CPython.

* pow(Number, Number, Number) - No proxies involved.
* pow(Number, Proxy, Number) - Fails with TypeError because pow() doesn't do type conversion on 2nd argument.
* pow(Number, Number, Proxy) - Fails with TypeError because pow() doesn't do type conversion on 3rd argument.

* pow(Proxy, Number, Number) - Will work as __pow__ in proxy can unwrap first argument.
* pow(Proxy, Proxy, Number) - Fails with TypeError because current __pow__ in proxy doesn't unwrap 2nd argument and underlying pow() doesn't do type conversion on 2nd argument.
* pow(Proxy, Number, Proxy) - Fails with TypeError because current __pow__ in proxy doesn't unwrap 3rd argument and underlying pow() doesn't do type conversion on 3rd argument.

For CPython C API.

* pow(Number, Proxy, Number) - Fails with TypeError because pow() doesn't do type conversion on 2nd argument. Nothing in proxy is called.
* pow(Number, Number, Proxy) - Fails with TypeError because pow() doesn't do type conversion on 3rd argument. Nothing in proxy is called.

* pow(Proxy, Number, Number) - Will work as __pow__ in proxy can unwrap first argument.
* pow(Proxy, Proxy, Number) - Currently works because proxy is unwrapping the 2nd argument before calling PyNumber_Power().
* pow(Proxy, Number, Proxy) - Fails with TypeError because proxy currently doesn't unwrap 3rd argument and underlying PyNumber_Power doesn't do type conversion on 3rd argument.

For PyPy.

* pow(Number, Number, Number) - No proxies involved.
* pow(Number, Proxy, Number) - Fails with TypeError because pow() doesn't do type conversion on 2nd argument.
* pow(Number, Number, Proxy) - Fails with TypeError because pow() doesn't do type conversion on 3rd argument.

* pow(Proxy, Number, Number) - Will work as __pow__ in proxy can unwrap first argument.
* pow(Proxy, Proxy, Number) - Fails with TypeError because current __pow__ in proxy doesn't unwrap 2nd argument and underlying pow() doesn't do type conversion on 2nd argument.
* pow(Proxy, Number, Proxy) - Fails with TypeError because current __pow__ in proxy doesn't unwrap 3rd argument and underlying pow() doesn't do type conversion on 3rd argument.

* pow(Number, Proxy, Number) - Will work as PyPy implements __rpow__ where as CPython doesn't, and proxy can unwrap second argument.
* pow(Number, Proxy, Proxy) - Fails with TypeError because __rpow__ in proxy doesn't unwrap 3rd argument and underlying pow() doesn't do type conversion on 3rd argument.

The conclusion I am coming to is that for consistency I can support unwrapping of 2nd or 3rd argument in CPython. This means changing the current C API code not to unwrap the second argument.

So for CPython, only thing that can be unwrapped is the first argument.

For PyPy, can still provide __rpow__ so it can unwrap second, but behaviour will then be different to CPython.

This is not going to be only case where manual unwrapping would be required. There are probably various builtin functions which don't support type conversion automatically.

Because I'm at the moment not focused to solve the SSL issue, I've taken the shortcut which is an "unverified security context":
Before in gspread2df.py:
```
    # auth for gspread
    gc = gspread.authorize(credentials)
```
Patched in gspread2df.py (relying on a patched gspread):
```
    import ssl
    # auth for gspread
    gc = gspread.authorize(credentials, ssl._create_unverified_context())
```

On 2018-06-22 06:46:54, Zack wrote:
> Sorry to pollute the *magic-wormhole* issue board with this, but I want to reply to @anarcat's claim.

Same here - I guess I'm the one who kept us drifting off-topic... ;)

>> i am not sure croc has the same security properties as wormhole. it seems more like an unprotected pastebin than a really secure transfer system
>
> These claims are a little unbased. While *croc* does not have the exact same security properties as *wormhole* it is secure. It uses AES-256 with pbkdf2-derived keys. It is not a pastebin, since the no content data is ever stored on the server which only acts as a relay (the server does store metadata, but this is also encrypted, and it is deleted from the server once the file transfer completes or after a specified time).

I guess I wasn't sure exactly how croc works. I'm not worried about the
AES-256 encryption, but I wonder how the keys are derived. In wormhole,
they are derived from the PAKE key exchange, but from what i can tell,
croc only implements a summary SHA256 hashed key exchange. Is the key
derived from that hash? Would that expose the content to a rainbow attack?

>> it's also too bad that it fragments the problem space
>
> I think this is not a good way to think about this. Having multiple solutions to the same problem is a good thing - it effectively creates a more robust general solution by providing alternatives and decentralizing the trust in a service. It also allows people building the tools to learn from one another and build from the best ideas (which is what I did when trying to create something similar to *magic-wormhole*).

I think it's great that people learn from each other as well, but I am
worried about reimplementations that diverge from the core
ideas. Wormhole's innovation is not as much the "encrypt a file and
store it on a server" but really the PAKE key exchange, which has
applications much beyond file sharing. In that respect, I found
@warner's talk at Pycon really enlightening:

https://youtu.be/oFrTqQw0_3c

I encourage you to consider implementing the wormhole protocol fully in
croc. :)

I'm going to close this one.. I greatly appreciate the interest, but I agree with exarkun that maintaining a PHP version of the Rendezvous Server represents more work than running multiple instances of the python-backed server.

Enhancing the client to correctly handle multiple servers (issue #72) *is* a worthwhile change, as we need it for ad-hoc networks (#48). But in that case, the new rendezvous server will be run by one or both of the client nodes, so it will need to be implemented in the same language, and I don't think a PHP version of the *client* is in the cards any time soon.

I think the "Transit Relay" component might be a better candidate for implementation in other languages. That's the part which is going to need scaling first, and the runtime cost is all bandwidth (the CPU and storage needs are minimal, but the relay server will spend all of its time ferrying bytes from one socket to another). So a PHP version might indeed help us take advantage of cheap servers which limit what languages you get to use. I wouldn't consider it to be a high priority, given how little servers cost these days, but it'd be a "nice to have".

This would be the code in https://github.com/warner/magic-wormhole/blob/0.9.2/src/wormhole/server/transit_server.py , which runs the protocol documented in https://github.com/warner/magic-wormhole/blob/master/docs/transit.md . I don't know much about PHP, but I suspect it wouldn't be too happy about the raw TCP sockets that the protocol uses right now. OTOH I think we may need to change that anyways (to make the relay usable by web-based clients that can only do HTTP).

As a module level function, I'd suggest something like `from_existing` or `from_instance`:

    new_obj = dataclasses.from_instance(existing_obj, field1=value1)

I like `from_instance` in particular, since the most explicit name for the operation would be `from_type_and_attributes`, and I think it's fair to say that the most importance role of an instance is to combine a particular type definition with a particular set of attribute values.
I think the existing PEP wording and examples are sufficient.

+1 for `dataclasses` having its own `ClassVar` equivalent as a fallback for when `typing` isn't available, *if* the decision is made to remove the `typing` module and it is deprecated.

-1 for `field(int, not_a_field=True)` or packing this into `field` in any other way. A class attr is arguably not a field. I think this would be inelegant and confusing, not only the syntax but e.g. `not_a_field=True` being incompatible with various other possible values of `field()` parameters.

I can't figure out why one would avoid just importing the `typing` module ... ?

If someone has a different module importable as `typing`, I think it is reasonable for that to cause problems, just like with any other module.

I encountered this while trying something like this:
```
for result in executor.map(somefunc, open("reallylargefilefile")):
    do_something_with_result()
```
The master process would then run out of memory building the list of futures because the file had upwards of a billion lines.
I just checked, and python 3.4 has exactly the same problem.

My first try was changing this:
`fs = [self.submit(fn, *args) for args in itertools.izip(*iterables)]`
Into this:
`fs = (self.submit(fn, *args) for args in itertools.izip(*iterables))`

That would have made fs an iterable, but then it wouldn't start anything until at least one job was fetched from the iterator. Breaking a previous fix.

So yes, it is heavy handed, and I'd like to be proven wrong about that. But it doesn't break when you feed it a large iterable.

Hi @limpkin and @phroekazoid the tcpNoDelay is already set to True as it is.

The WebSocketClientFactory base class sets it True by default. You can inspect the source here, note the `self.resetProtocolOptions()` call
https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/7ccee1b4e70c3fc189d5bfb6fe8fa500647f6fa7/autobahn/websocket/protocol.py#L3764

The error is python based not the binance library. Your code is built using recursion and python has limits on recursion to prevent stack overflow. You can increase the recursion limit (not advised) using: 
```
import sys
limit= ?
sys.setrecursionlimit(limit)
```
Per documentation: 
> Set the maximum depth of the Python interpreter stack to limit. This limit prevents infinite recursion from causing an overflow of the C stack and crashing Python.
> 
> The highest possible limit is platform-dependent. A user may need to set the limit higher when she has a program that requires deep recursion and a platform that supports a higher limit. This should be done with care, because a too-high limit can lead to a crash.

Id advise rewriting your function without recursion. If you're trying to create a loop that executes after a given amount of time you can use the time library and set a time.sleep() inside a while loop.
Additionally if its a matter of getting historical tickers just use the get_historical_klines function and set your specified time.

The basic idea was to make most of the built-in activation functions have most of their "interesting" behavior occur [in the same region](http://neat-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/activation.html).  That way if you are evolving a network that has multiple different possible activation functions, a mutation which changes the activation function won't require a drastic change in the inputs to be in a region where the activation function isn't saturated.

If you need to use the standard sigmoid function, you can always [add your own without a huge amount of trouble](http://neat-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/customization.html#new-activation-functions).

Please take a look at the README if you have recently updated the mailchimp3 plugin as version 2.x is vastly different from 1.x. In general, most delete commands in the MailChimp API return an HTTP 204 (No Content) header, with the expectedly blank body. The List Member commands can be found [here](http://developer.mailchimp.com/documentation/mailchimp/reference/lists/members/), with the delete performing this way. For any delete command that receives a 204 status code, we do not return anything, otherwise you will receive a JSON object of the data provided in the return.

@prokher some comments on your note.

> I think that the restriction that all threads must be greenthreads is too restrictive.

There is no such restriction. I personally don't think it is a good idea, but I don't think there is a problem with mixing green and regular threads in the same process. The green threads will obviously run all in the same physical thread context, but you can have additional threads if you want.

> Consider you have a working thread which performs mathematical operation using numpy.

Putting heavy CPU tasks in a Python web server is a bad idea. Even if the GIL is released, I think you are looking for trouble. Much better to keep things separate by running one or a pool of workers for the CPU intensive tasks (for example using Celery). Then you are not bound to eventlet/gevent and can run those workers without any of the considerations required to run under these frameworks. As a benefit, you can scale the servers and the workers separately, since they may have different loads.

> When math calculations finish I need to somehow notify the eventloop where socket io server lives in

These days this Socket.IO server supports multiple server nodes, and also emitting from non-server processes (such as these CPU intensive workers I proposed above). So this isn't really a problem. The only requirement to have these multi-process features is to run a message queue such as RabbitMQ or Redis that all these processes use to coordinate efforts.

Hello Mohammad,

it seems, that you send directly a rpc method call *countProjects* to your TL server. This will confuse the TL server, because this method is not declared as an supported rpc api inside his php sources.

 *countProjects* is a method only know by the python class *TestlinkAPIClient*, which send a *getProjects* RPC call to the TL server and evaluates the responds.

My suggestion is, please take a look into the example https://github.com/lczub/TestLink-API-Python-client/blob/master/example/TestLinkExample.py#L683
It shows, how you can initiate the  python api client and communicate with the TL server.

Hope this helps,
Regards Luiko

*UPDATE*: On a closer inspection it seems like `@shared_task` in Celery 3 and 4 specific and the Celery support can be checked with the same code base for them:

http://docs.celeryproject.org/en/3.1/django/first-steps-with-django.html?highlight=shared_task
http://docs.celeryproject.org/en/v4.0.2/django/first-steps-with-django.html?highlight=shared_task

But Celery 2 actually uses the `@task` decorator that is available from `celery.task`:

http://docs.celeryproject.org/en/2.5-archived/reference/celery.app.task.html?highlight=app.task#module-celery.app.task

Since Celery 2 is actually the latent version that does not receive any updates, perhaps we should do the following renames for the next release:

- Rename `health_check_celery` to `health_check_celery2` OR deprecate the old health checker AND
- Rename `health_check_celery3` to `health_check_celery` as it supports both Celery 3 and Celery 4. This way it is easier to understand that Celery 3 and 4 are actively supported and Celery 2 is the version that is specially treated.

I do see a problem with your command line:

In our [YARN instructions](https://github.com/yahoo/TensorFlowOnSpark/wiki/GetStarted_YARN), we [create a Python.zip file](https://github.com/yahoo/TensorFlowOnSpark/wiki/GetStarted_YARN#create-a-python-w-tensorflow-zip-package-for-spark) that contains the TensorFlow dependencies.  We then ship this Python.zip file to the executors, because the Hadoop nodes will typically have a different version of Python or won't have the TensorFlow dependencies pre-installed.

When we ship the Python.zip via `--archives`, we also provide a "directory name" to unzip into (on the executors).  In our case, we use `--archives hdfs:///user/${USER}/Python.zip#Python`, which means "on the executors, unzip into a directory called 'Python'".

We then tell the Spark executors where to find this zipped version of Python via: `export SPARK_YARN_USER_ENV="PYSPARK_PYTHON=Python/bin/python"`.  Note that the `Python` prefix here should match the `#Python` suffix of the `--archives` above.  So, it looks like you should change your `SPARK_YARN_USER_ENV`.

And finally, if that still doesn't work, please capture the yarn logs via `yarn logs -applicationId <your_application_id>  > yarn.log`.

Something to think about: `Translator.translate()` takes a type, and tries to translate it. If it fails, it'll return `BaseObject`. Then, the usual procedure is to pass `session, object_id, response_object` to the class that is returned.

This has two problems:
- Ideally, the default return value would be either `BaseObject` or `APIJSONObject`, depending on the object. I would choose `BaseObject` if there were an `id`, and `APIJSONObject` otherwise. However, we can't do that since the method only accepts type. We'd need to make a backwards-incompatible change to make it accept additional information.
- The types of classes we return have different `__init__` parameters. So you can't initialize an object unless you know in advance which type it's going to be. We could fix this by:
  - Moving the object creation inside of `Translator.translate()` (after dealing with the above problem).
  - Making `APIJSONObject.__init__` have the same argument list as `BaseObject.__init__`, even though it doesn't need most of them.

Let's not worry about this right now. What we have now works well enough to push what you have as-is. But we'll want to revisit this before or during work on #140.

a small recap for everybody:
mssql4 uses features available from 2012 onwards to allow faster paging: if you're on 2012,2014,2016, mssql4 is the way to go, no questions asked.
mssql3 uses a trick similar to what oracle does (wrapping the actual set in an inner query), but it can be used from 2005 onwards (so 2005, 2008, 2008R2 in addition, of course, to 2012,2014,2016)
mssql is for everything older than 2005, so REEEEEALLLY not to use if not strictly required.

the "n" bit (mssql3n, mssql4n) is if you want to use unicode everywhere, which should be the case for all modern apps.

If I understand well, your need is similar to a one-to-one RNN structure in which the first 50 outputs are ignored. 

If you're not already using it, I think it would be worth looking at the `mx.symbol.RNN` which provides a convenient abstraction for working with of recurrent structure. In short, it takes as input a data of dims batch_size x sequence_length and its output is of dims hidden_features x batch_size x sequence_length. For your need, you could slice that output to remove the first 50 elements of the sequence_length dimension and then proceed to the decoding and application of the output layer as usual. 

Not sure if the model might crash because some elements of the RNN outputs end nowhere because of the slicing. If it's the case, it could do a dummy trick such as multiplying by 0 these first 50 outputs and merge back to the rest for decoding. 

I don't have code that do exactly that, but I'm currently looking to bring that RNN structure into the general R API, you can take a look at the WIP here so see how to deal with `mx.symbol.RNN`: https://github.com/jeremiedb/mxnet_R_bucketing/blob/master/blog/Blog_LSTM_NLP_Classification.Rmd

3. The deployment currently in the code-base is for the travis tests so it doesn't really require a custom deployment. I think integrating it with helm is a great idea.

4. The pods vs. jobs discussion has gone on for a while. The problem is that jobs will run repeatedly until they pass. This could be potentially disastrous in the context of an airflow job (imagine you have a script that launches a spark job that keeps failing).

@Fokko @bolkedebruin apologies that there has been some delay on the final steps. I was waiting for https://github.com/bloomberg/airflow/pull/44 to be rebased so I could merge it before we merge, but I think this can wait and @fenglu-g can PR against master once the main PR is merged. I will do the final rebases and clean up requested so we can start the alpha testing.

Here is a decorator example to take benefit from the PR

``
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
 
from functools import wraps
import os
 
def worker_process_is_group_leader(func):
    """
    A modified version of the "billiard" library cleanly kills the whole process group.
    But it requires having the worker process as the process group leader
    """
    @wraps(func)
    def decorated_function(*args, **kwargs):
        os.setpgrp()
        return func(*args, **kwargs)
 
    return decorated_function
``

How would a client know why the catalog settings are not updated? This seems to eat the error silently.
The catalog info is pulled by the two lines preceding the try block. So any user with access to the catalog will be able to see it's updated info. Currently users who doesn't have permission to view the ACL of the catalog, are unable to see even the basic info about the catalog. vcd catalog info leads to an error message stating that they don't have access to the catalog. And this error stems from the fact that the second call to fetch ACL fails for those users. Please note, the first call is not the reason of the failure. The current fix is for only those aforementioned users. Ideally we should have the option to determine if we should fetch the ACL of the catalog or not , based on the fact that the user has access to those settings or not. I am not aware of any api endpoint that hands us out that info. So as a workaround we query for the ACL regardless of who the user is, if the query fails, we don't bubble up the specific exception viz. AccessForbiddenException and exclude ACL info section from the output. Users who has access to the ACL setting will remain unaffected. It's by design that we are eating away the specific exception related to permission. If any other exception is raised during the process those will bubble up as usual. --- *[vcd_cli/catalog.py, line 114 at r1](https://reviewable.io/reviews/vmware/vcd-cli/225#-LFQkPyT1AayEqR-3Y6f:-LFTDZ0WBcgTkYvIrXCb:b-896fix) ([raw file](https://github.com/vmware/vcd-cli/blob/2ca82e3d43241c406acdfd234c507568af0d1d20/vcd_cli/catalog.py#L114)):*

to 

`context = ssl._create_unverified_context()`

Warning this is basically the verify/false option active.

Hope this helps and it will be fixed soon :-)

> How can I figure out if I set the --touch-eject flag is set or not? (ykman mode does not seem to tell me) Same for the two timeouts that can be set via ykman mode: How can I read the current settings ?

No, these flags can not be read from the device once written. However, the behaviour of the device should change when `--touch-eject` is set, you should be able to eject/insert the smartcard by pressing the button. The LED should indicate if the card is inserted or not as well.

> Can I have Yubico OTP configured on slot 1 AND be able to use Challenge Response (yubico mode) with this slot 1 ?? (ykman slot calculate 1 4a6fbb fails for me)
Or will I need a second slot to have Yubico OTP /and/ Challenge Response (ykchalresp) ??

A slot has _either_ a Yubico OTP or a challenge-response credential configured. So a Yubico OTP in slot 1 and a challenge response secret in slot 2 should work fine.

> More general:
You wrote: """These slots can have one of the following credentials configured: a Yubico OTP (which is what comes preconfigured in the short press slot on a new key), a static password, a challenge-response credential, a OATH-HOTP credential. """ Is it "you can have one AND ONLY ONE" or "one or multiple" of the following configured (on a single slot) ? - So can I combine any of these ? As OTP, OATH-HOTP and Static password are all TOUCH triggered (Challenge response credential cannot be triggered through button touch, or??), they are probably mutually exclusive. But as Challenge-Response is triggered via API calls, this could potentially be combined, no ?

One, and only one, type of credential per slot. A challenge-response credential may require touch, see `$ ykman slot chalresp -h`.

> The LED of my key is shortly flashing every two-three seconds; constantly. Is that because CCID mode is activated? What the is the benefit of this blinking? Can I disable or suppress the LED (without the CCID mode) under Linux?

I believe the best current workaround to get the LED to blink less is to disable CCID.

> The more I learn about yk4 I think ykman is well thought and it's command structure makes sense and also will help users to better understand all the possibilities.

> How can this be? - I mean the first "nothing to do..." is possible, even though I thought I had set my yubikey to mode 86 the last time. But then the ykman mode 86 shoudl have something to do (changing the touch-eject flag), no ??

The only valid modes when using numbers is 0 - 6 (see https://github.com/Yubico/yubikey-manager/blob/master/ykman/util.py#L94). The extra flags are not part of the mode in that sense, they just need to be set at the same time as the mode is set. 86 is not a valid mode. It might be a bug that the tool accepts higher values.

There's also [this](https://github.com/Yubico/yubikey-manager-qt/blob/master/ykman-gui/py/logging_setup.py) which looks to be replicating things.

`py` is the python finder. It uses special methods spelled out in pep 514 to search for python executables. Since `pipenv run` doesn’t alter your environment as thoroughly as `pipenv shell`, the pep-514 style finder will not know where to search for libraries besides the normal places. That is to say, `pipenv run` simply puts `virtualenv\Scripts` at the head of your `PATH` environment variable. The windows finder doesn’t even look there to execute things, so it will never be affected by this. 

With pipenv, you’ve already set up a virtual environment with a python executable in it. So you shouldn’t use the finder, you should just use `pipenv run python <script>`

OK, I've now read your reply more fully and I see why you're finding the current behaviour difficult to follow. Let me try to clarify.

> * Your perspective seems to be that--in a global installation--a subprocess Python would use the system Python, thus, in a virtual environment the subprocess Python should also use the system Python.
> * My perspective is that in a global installation a subprocess Python would use the same Python as its parent and so in a virtual environment the subprocess Python should also use the same Python as its parent.

This is the first point where I think your view is going astray. There's no such thing as "a subprocess Python". All there is, is "a subprocess". Python's subprocess module simply calls the OS to start a new process (`execv` on Unix, `CreateProcess` on Windows). Whether that subprocess runs Python, a shell, or anything else, is entirely down to the OS, and Python has no interest in, or control over, what happens.

When you're talking about a "Python subprocess", your examples are scripts that start with a shebang line `#!/usr/bin/env python`. Those are handled by the OS, which on Unix reacts to the first 2 bytes `#!` and reads the line, invoking the executable `/usr/bin/env` to run Python - which it does by searching the `PATH` environment variable for a python executable (because that's what the `env` program does). The one it finds is the system Python, because you've done nothing to tell it to do anything different. (The situation on Windows is somewhat different, but I'll ignore that here). I don't know if I'm explaining things you already know - if so, I apologise - but the net effect is that the Python program that invokes the subprocess has *no control* over what the OS does, except by the normal means of setting environment variables like `PATH`. (The Python program calling `subprocess` could modify the `PATH` environment variable if it chose to, but the Python *language* certainly shouldn't do so without being asked).

> As I understand it, when an virtual environment is "activated":
> 1. The correct Python library search paths for the virtual environment are set (via sys.path); and,
> 2. The PATH (and other environment variables) are modified to ensure the correct Python binary (i.e. the one within the virtual environment) is used.

Not correct.

Specifically activation doesn't do (1) at all. The standard Python executable sets `sys.path` and it does so based on various things (the location of the executable). The `site.py` file shipped with virtualenv does further things to modify this. But *none* of this is dependent on activation - it happens for all virtualenvs.

What activation does is (1) add the `bin` directory of the virtualenv to the front of `$PATH`, and (2) set the `VIRTUAL_ENV` environment variable. Apart from some cosmetics (setting the prompt) that's it. And of those, the *only* change that has any global effect is the setting of `PATH` (i.e., unless a program explicitly checks if `VIRTUAL_ENV` is set, it won't care).

So, the specific effect of all this is that the *only* practical difference activating a virtual environment has is in how `PATH` is set.

Whether your scripts are written to assume that `python` is a command available on `PATH`, and it points to the "correct" Python, is a matter for those scripts to get right. It sounds like the Ansible scripts you're referring to do make that assumption, but the Homebrew approach of "using a virtual environment" is somehow violating that assumption if you don't activate the virtualenv. That's an issue that will need to be resolved between those two parties - it's not something that virtualenv can (or should, in my opinion) "fix". The resolution may be as simple as saying "running the Homebrew scripts without activating the virtualenv is not supported" or it may be something complex like patching the scripts - I can't say.

In my view, the real confusion here comes from the term "activating". It *sounds* like it's a really clever bit of machinery, but it isn't - it's just adding the virtualenv to `PATH`. You don't install git and then "activate" it, you simply add the directory you pit the git executable in to your `PATH` (or more likely, git installs itself to somewhere that's by convention already on your `PATH`, but you know what I mean...)

I hope this helps clarify.

> This is an instance of an anti-pattern I've identified - let's call it the
> "quasiglobal" anti-pattern - where you have an API which maintains some
> global state, but then, *if you like*, you can opt out of that global
> state by passing a parameter along. Superficially this enables easier
> testing and such, but it creates an obvious division between "normal" code
> and "testable" code.
>
> It's an anti-pattern because it leads to a problem where A calls B and B
> calls C and C calls D, and A, B, and C all take the quasiglobal parameter
> (in this case, agent, in Twisted's case, the other instance of this
> anti-pattern, reactor) but D doesn't, and so you have to modify D to
> actually pass things all the way through.
>
> What I think we should do longer-term is to get rid of the treq module as
> an interface, and instead, make HTTPClient the interface that you
> interact with; making the treq module simply a global instance of that
> API.

@jni Frankly I would disagree that nested loops hurts readability. To me, aggressively vectorized functions and other clever methods usually end up being much harder to follow. I don't have a problem with them, but just doubt readability is a valid criticism.

With a little tweaking of the whitespace and comments, I find it hard to believe this could be easier to follow/maintain with any other algorithmic structure. The added bonus is that looping in this manner generally is how papers describe algorithms, so it's easier to compare and search for potential discrepancies vs. the original publications.

``` python 
# Iterate over rows, taking padding into account
for row in range(offset, n_row + offset):
    row_start = row - offset
    row_end = row + offset + 1
    
    # Iterate over columns, taking padding into account
    for col in range(offset, n_col + offset):
        col_start = col - offset
        col_end = col + offset + 1
    	
    	# Iterate over channels
        for color in range(n_ch):
            new_values[color] = 0
            
        # Reset weights for each local region
        weight_sum = 0
        
        # Iterate over local 2d patch for each pixel
        # First rows
        for i in range(max(-d, offset - row),
                       min(d + 1, n_row + offset - row)):
            row_start_i = row_start + i
            row_end_i = row_end + i
            
            # Local patch columns
            for j in range(max(-d, offset - col),
                           min(d + 1, n_col + offset - col)):
                col_start_j = col_start + j
                col_end_j = col_end + j
                
                # Shortcut for grayscale, else assume RGB
                if n_ch == 1:
                    weight = patch_distance_2d(
                             padded[row_start:row_end,
                                    col_start:col_end, 0],
                             padded[row_start_i:row_end_i,
                                    col_start_j:col_end_j, 0],
                             w, s)
                else:
                    weight = patch_distance_2drgb(
                             padded[row_start:row_end,
                                    col_start:col_end, :],
                             padded[row_start_i:row_end_i,
                                    col_start_j:col_end_j, :],
                                    w, s)
                
                # Collect results in weight sum
                weight_sum += weight
                
                # Apply to each channel multiplicatively
                for color in range(n_ch):
                    new_values[color] += weight * padded[row + i,
                                                         col + j, color]
                                                         
        # Normalize the result
        for color in range(n_ch):
            result[row, col, color] = new_values[color] / weight_sum
            
# Return cropped result, undoing padding
return result[offset:-offset, offset:-offset]
```

@breznak point by point:

* The range-select is incompatible with the mouse-zoom feature. It is either one or the other (unless we want to see if we can make a change to DyGraphs - which I don't want to try right now). Personally, I prefer the mouse-zoom feature.
* For dealing with CSV files that might have a different name than "timestamp", for now, I suggest we leave it, requiring the user to have a field named, "timestamp". In a later iteration, we can pop up a modal that says something like, "We noticed that your data has no 'timestamp' field. Would you like to select the field that represents the time data?" and show a list of the fields. After doing this, the user can then click the "Render" button.
* I will look into the error that occurs on CSV files that do have timestamps, but aren't able to be rendered.
*  DyGraphs requires that all rows have a valid Javascript date object. In Javascript, we create a Date object in this format: `new Date(year, month[, day[, hour[, minutes[, seconds[, milliseconds]]]]]);` So, if we pass in a float, it won't really know what to do with that, as that would indicate a floating point year. This means we will have to write some logic that will handle a variety of submissions. I am not sure how to do this other than to write rules for specific types of timestamps (integers, floats, etc.). Alternatively, we could make it accept *only* ISO 8601 formatted strings (which would be the sane thing to do, but isn't compatible with the examples you have given me). Right now, I have some logic in there that will accept dates in the following formats:
 * yyyy:mm:dd hh:mm:ss:ms
 * mm/dd/yyyy hh:mm:ss:ms (American style)

Almost ready for integration. Just the tiny comment regarding signal registration and change to use the Invenio-Indexer task instead of providing its own.

1) The bulk indexer includes the ``_version`` and ``_version_type= external_gte`` which means that a record will be indexed if its version number is greater than or equal to the current version number in ES. I.e. if we first index a new version of a record and then an old version, the old version will simply be discarded from the index. If we index twice the same record version, then ES will also index it twice. If that's not desired the version type can be changed to ``external_gt`` for a specific indexing session.

2) I'm not sure it's worthwhile the effort. As you've correctly tried it's the rabbitmq [message iterator](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-indexer/blob/master/invenio_indexer/api.py#L193) which should be changed to not include the same record twice, but often you can have multiple indexing processes running at the same time, so a record added twice in the queue could potentially be indexed from two different worker nodes.

3) Do you have some stats on performance of Record.get_records vs Record.get_record when indexing?

No, I don't think there is any issue open. The confusion stems from the [`<uses-sdk />` tag in AndroidManifest.xml](http://developer.android.com/guide/topics/manifest/uses-sdk-element.html), which specifies the API levels for your application.

So in p4a old-toolchain, `--sdk` specifies the target API for your application, and `--minsdk` sets the minimum API. But in buildozer.spec, `android.api` and `android.minapi` set those values, while `android.sdk` is the actual SDK version and `android.minsdk` doesn't exist.

``` python
from kivy.app import App
from kivy.lang import Builder
from android import AndroidService

class MyApp(App):

    service = None
    labels = ["hello", "world"]
    flag = False

    def build(self):
        return root

    def start_service(self):
        if not self.service:
            self.service = AndroidService("Python For Android Test Service", "running")
            self.service.start(self.labels[int(self.flag)])
            self.flag = not self.flag

    def stop_service(self):
        if self.service:
            self.service.stop()
            self.service = None

    def on_stop(self):
        self.stop_service()

root = Builder.load_string("""
BoxLayout:

    Button:
        text: "Start"
        on_release:
            app.start_service()
    Button:
        text: "Stop"
        on_release:
            app.stop_service()        
""")

MyApp().run()

it's correct that content-type isn't mandatory: http://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec7.html#sec7.2.1

the thing i'm not sure about is whether we should return `None` or `''` or just raise an exception right here... what speaks against `''` is the handling of `content_type` in [Graph.parse()](https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/blob/master/rdflib/graph.py#L1031). If we return `''` it won't enter the default handling of `if format is None`... but then the default handling would be wrong in this case and the comments there show that we should maybe be explicit and raise an exception.

So if at all, i'd rather return `None`:

```diff
         self.content_type = file.info().get('content-type')
-        self.content_type = self.content_type.split(";", 1)[0]
+        if self.content_type is not None:
+             self.content_type = self.content_type.split(";", 1)[0]
```

ok, so as you might've noticed we're nearing an end wrt. #443. As the new parser changes the interface and returns different results when parsing the same file, i have to make it a new major release of rdflib in terms of http://semver.org ... i'm not afraid of bumping that number, but as you might have noticed, this causes me to move a lot of issues to milestone 6.0.0 (such as this one), as they on their own have the potential to change rdflib in a backwards incompatible way.

As a new major release is a signal to system maintainers that things changed in a backwards incompatible fashion, doing this too often is not really good style. It's also a very strong reason to externalize individual changing parsers into sub-packages again, allowing to use old parsers with an updated rdflib core to not break your parsing results...

The obvious downside of that is that splitting causes a lot of redundant work (making sub-packages, setting up travis, where to run the tests, specifying which version combinations are supported, etc.) and divides the few developers even further.

I'm still undecided what's best here, also "best" relies on how often upstream format specs change in a backwards incompatible way... let's hope they rarely do and this was an exception...

There is one other detail that I thought of.  In Python, a dict doesn't keep any particular order, so the order of the output columns won't necessarily match the order given in the yaml file.  This is probably ok in most cases, especially if the output file is a FITS binary table.  These are usually accessed by column name, so it doesn't matter what order they are in.  

However, some users may desire a particular output order, especially for ASCII output files where it is more common to access columns by number.  In this case, I think we can let the user provide the desired order of the columns in a list item, called 'order' perhaps:
```
output :
    truth :
        file_name : ...
        columns : 
            ...
        order : [ 'num', 'x', 'y', 'g1', 'g2', 'psf_e1', 'psf_e2' ]
```
We could either require that this list has all the column names given in the 'columns' item, or fill in any missing items at the end of the list.  I'm not sure which is more user friendly.  :)

i agree that we should tackle module per module; the only requirement here should be that py2 keeps working.
but i wouldn't put my hopes up too much that we have so much code that can be replaced.

EB is however not a good testcase, quite a lot of code in vsc-base is not used in EB (eg vsc.utils.run), and not unittested (enough).
the easiest way to proceed with testing is to make 2 scripts:
1. one that clones and installs all our repos (public and private) (`python setup.py install --user`, no tests needed) [this might require some dependency resolution, but that's probably not hard to orden]
2. a script that runs `puython setup.py test` on all these clones

make sure the script works with master first.
now when someone makes a PR in vsc-base to fix something, go to the vsc-base clone, pull the PR and install it; and then run the script to run all tests

getting the repos in order, and maybe some easy_install to install the extra test dependencies is probably the largest amount of work. testing with py27 in an el7 box is also more than enough. eb should at some point decide when to drop py26, but i guess el6 has a few more years ahead.

when this is done, i have no objections to merge stuff that includes heavy rewrites. if not, i'll probably review things in detail, and revert PRs that i don't like merged (like https://github.com/hpcugent/vsc-base/pull/265) without full testing. but i have little time, so waiting for my reviews will drag out this effort a lot.

the main reason not to ship them is that they are not part of the code. if convenience is so mportant, why don't you also ship all possible python binaries and all their dependencies with EB, will also make things easier.
if you are debugging something, you should accept the fact that you might need to install extra things simply to debug the specific issue, and once it's fixed, you shouldn't need it anymore.
i think that by shipping the unittests, and running them as part of EB, you are using the EB unittests as a way to verify the system EB runs on, rather then unittests of EB itself.

> Stolon has a built-in proxy server which allows me to proxy all my UPDATE, DELETE and INSERT queries to the master, and SELECT queries to slaves. 

AFAIK it can't work as LAYER 7 proxy.

> Patroni doesn't have this useful feature.

You can configure HAProxy to load-balance read-only traffic across available replicas: https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/master/extras/confd/templates/haproxy.tmpl#L26-L32
Off course it will require you to have two connection urls in the application.

> Pgpool allows you to split read and write queries and proxy them to different nodes. 

Unfortunately it is not very reliable, because SELECT statements are able to modify data if they call VOLATILE functions.

> Here is a simple proof of concept:

I would suggest to use [confd](https://github.com/kelseyhightower/confd) for that. Its main goal to generate configuration files from templates and keep them in sync with information from DCS.  It already supports etcd, consul and zookeeper.

We already have an example of generating HAProxy configuration with the help of confd: https://github.com/zalando/patroni/tree/master/extras/confd
And [here](https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/master/docker/entrypoint.sh#L34-L45) is an example how to start confd.

postgres.yaml:
```yaml
postgresql:
  parameters:
    archive_mode: "on"
    archive_command: envdir "/path/to/env-dir" wal-e wal-push "%p"
  recovery_conf:
    restore_command: envdir "/path/to/env-dir" wal-e wal-fetch "%f" "%p"
```

Although don't forget to create a cronjob, which will do `envdir "/path/to/env-dir" wal-e backup-push "/path/to/postgres/data/directory"

Please read about envdir in wal-e documentation: https://github.com/wal-e/wal-e/

I think it's not the task of Patroni. One can always implement a delay by supplying custom recovery.conf parameter to Patroni configuration and designating the node as nofailover and noloadbalance. I'm closing this issue.

> Reinit and restart on the API just time out. 

It's kind of strange. 
Reinit is performed asynchronously and API should respond immediately that Reinitialize is either scheduled or started.

Restart in this case is useless but it should not time out either.

> When is it safe to call reap_children?

It is safe to call it from the Main thread. Most important there should be no subprocess hanging around, which return code is important to get. I think `is_pid_running` should be relatively good place for it.

```python
@@ -13,7 +13,7 @@ import time
 
 from collections import defaultdict
 from patroni.exceptions import PostgresConnectionException, PostgresException
-from patroni.utils import compare_values, parse_bool, parse_int, Retry, RetryFailedError, polling_loop
+from patroni.utils import compare_values, parse_bool, parse_int, Retry, RetryFailedError, polling_loop, reap_children
 from six import string_types
 from threading import current_thread, Lock, Event
 
@@ -603,11 +603,12 @@ class Postgresql(object):
             logger.warning("Garbage pid in postmaster.pid: {0!r}".format(pid))
             return 0
 
-    @staticmethod
-    def is_pid_running(pid):
+    def is_pid_running(self, pid):
         try:
             if pid < 0:
                 pid = -pid
+            if current_thread().ident == self.__thread_ident:
+                reap_children()
             return pid > 0 and pid != os.getpid() and pid != os.getppid() and (os.kill(pid, 0) or True)
         except Exception:
             return False
```

Hi @angelbarrera92 sorry a bit late on the response here.

You've got the general idea right. We use SWAG as a central repository for account metadata. We have 100s of accounts, with so many accounts even simple things like naming become a challenge, one tool says something is prod, another says it's prod-staging, etc. which is right? We use SWAG to resolve and disambiguate these problems, at the end of day most tools just need an account number, unfortunately 12 char strings are very human friendly. SWAG attempts to bridge that gap. 

Additionally, SWAG can store information for infrastructure services that need to store account level metadata. 

Again the above is all up to you to under, SWAG just provides a schema for organizing this data and some ways to store and retrieve that information. 

We do have #22 open that might help you pre populate some of this data if you're using AWS organizations. But SWAG is not strictly AWS specific and could be used for other cloud providers.

Hope this helps!

Chalice can't build pyyaml unfortunately. Their `setup.py` fails to detect when using a compiler to compile their C speedups fails, which means that you can't build a proper wheel for it outside of linux. They also don't provide pre-built wheels, which exacerbates the issue.

What you'll need to do is build the wheel on a linux instance (ideally amazon linux), and then vendor that built wheel.

The good news is that there's a fix to the PyYAML `setup.py` that's awaiting release.

Feel free to add new interface for the recorder as you see fit. Right now the recorder has https://github.com/aws/aws-xray-sdk-python/blob/master/aws_xray_sdk/core/recorder.py#L287 `capture` function so that any normal function can be annotated to be recorded. Once you add a `record_subsegment_async` helper a new function `capture_async` can utilize this helper so that all async functions can be easily recorded. 

You probably have to do some work around compat.py so that the new change doesn't break python2.7. You cannot do `import asyncio` on top of `recorder.py`.

@xamoom-raphael : Just a heads up, the conversation here has gotten a bit theoretical when I think you just want to be able to read your data. Sorry about that.

Hang tight while we try to figure something out... If you have any ideas, chime in, but we have to be careful that whatever we do to deal with this situation doesn't give a "false sense of security" or overly complicate things under the hood.
